context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on aid and development. i'm kinda stuck on how to compare Easterly vs Sachs and tie in the moral/effectiveness stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's get you unstuck. Before we dive into the comparison, tell me: what do you already know about Easterly's and Sachs's positions on aid? Even a rough sense of where each one stands will help us figure out where to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the process. I should orient them to the assignment without doing any of the intellectual work for them. Rather than explaining the comparison, I'll ask them a probing opening question to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: tbh my rough take is Easterly thinks big top-down aid mostly fails, and Sachs is way more pro-aid and thinks it can solve poverty if scaled up right. idk if that’s too simplistic tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point, not too simplistic at all. Now push it a little further: what do you think each of them gets *right*, and where does each one go *too far*? That tension is really the heart of what the prompt is asking you to explore.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable rough take that is essentially correct. I should validate the direction without over-praising, and then push them to go deeper with a Socratic question that moves toward the assignment's core tensions. The prompt asks whether Easterly is 'overly dismissive' and whether Sachs is 'guilty of the opposite sin' - so I want to nudge the student toward evaluating the strengths and weaknesses of each position, not just describing them.

exchanges.student: i think Easterly gets it right that aid can be wasteful, paternalistic, and bad at seeing local context. but maybe he goes too far if he acts like aid is mostly doomed, and Sachs maybe goes too far the other way by being kinda overconfident about what money + planning can do.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you're identifying real weaknesses in both positions. Now the prompt also asks about short-term vs long-term aid. Do you think that distinction changes how you evaluate either argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building good critical thinking about both authors. This is solid progress. Now I should push them toward the next part of the prompt - the short-term vs long-term aid distinction, and/or the moral case for aid - to keep the conversation moving toward a complete reflection rather than dwelling on the Easterly/Sachs comparison.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think so. like emergency/humanitarian aid seems easier to defend, but long-term development aid feels messier since institutions, politics, and local power matter way more.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction. So given that, what do you think the *moral* case for aid actually is? Does it differ depending on whether we're talking short-term or long-term?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good intuitive distinction. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect this insight to the moral case for aid, which is another key prompt question they haven't addressed yet. This will help move the conversation toward a more complete reflection.

exchanges.student: i think morally, short-term aid is easier bc if ppl are starving or displaced, not helping feels wrong fr. long-term aid still has a moral case imo, but only if it supports local agency instead of outsiders deciding everything.
exchanges.tutor: Strong point about local agency - that connects well to bigger course themes. One more angle the prompt raises: what happens to aid when it gets tied to security agendas, like counter-insurgency efforts? How does that complicate the moral case you just made?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a coherent moral framework that connects well to the course themes of legitimacy, power, and local agency. Good progress. I should now push them toward the last major prompt question they haven't addressed: the transformation of aid in light of security agendas and counter-insurgency. This is a distinct and important thread they need to engage with for a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: ngl that part confuses me a bit. i guess once aid is tied to security, it stops being just about helping people and starts serving donor states’ political or military goals too?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So if aid is serving the donor's security interests, what does that do to the trust and legitimacy of aid in the eyes of the people receiving it? And how might that affect its effectiveness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right instinct. I should affirm it briefly and then push them to think about the specific implication - how does this compromise the effectiveness and moral legitimacy of aid? This connects back to their earlier point about local agency and will help them tie threads together for their reflection.

exchanges.student: then ppl might see it as manipulation, not solidarity, which hurts legitimacy. and if trust drops, the aid prob works worse bc local communities may resist it or not really buy in.
exchanges.tutor: You've now got solid ideas covering all the main angles of the prompt. I think it's time to stop discussing and start writing. Try drafting your reflection using what we've worked through - don't worry about it being perfect. What's your opening sentence or main argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all the major prompt questions: Easterly vs Sachs, short vs long-term aid, moral case for aid, and security agendas affecting legitimacy and effectiveness. They have enough material to write their reflection. It's time to push them toward integrating everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss concepts. This is consistent with the rule about sensing redundancy and asking for integration after sufficient discussion.

exchanges.student: maybe my main argument is: aid can be necessary, esp in the short term, but long-term development aid is only defensible and effective when it’s politically realistic, locally accountable, and not captured by donor security agendas.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis - it's specific and takes a real position. Now go write the draft. Come back when you have something on paper and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a strong, clear thesis that integrates the main threads of the prompt. I should affirm it briefly and push them to start actually writing rather than continuing to refine the thesis in chat. The goal now is to get them drafting.

exchanges.student: ok, i’ll draft it now. can i come back after w/ like a rough intro if it sounds awkward?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, bring it back - but just a heads up: I won't edit or polish your writing for you. What I can do is help you think through whether your ideas are clear and well-reasoned. You do the writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help polishing their writing, which falls outside my role - I must not edit, revise, or suggest language. I should clarify my boundaries while still encouraging them to bring their draft back for discussion of ideas.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s fine, i mostly need a clarity check tbh. i'll send the intro once i write it.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good - go write it and come back when you're ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands the boundaries and is ready to write. I should give a brief, encouraging send-off and wait for their draft. No need to add anything substantive here.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas were drawn from the student through targeted questions rather than being supplied by the tutor. The tutor built progressively on each student response, scaffolded the short-term/long-term distinction, the moral case, and the security-agenda complication in sequence, and explicitly declined to edit or polish the student's writing in Turn 9. No submission-ready content was provided, no direct answers were given, and the dialogue advanced efficiently without redundancy. Tone was consistently supportive and appropriately formative.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. The tutor never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them (1.1.C), never produced near-submission-ready text (1.1.A), and explicitly refused editing help (1.1.B). Scaffolding built logically from the student's existing knowledge through each prompt angle (1.2). The tutor gave process-level feedback by noting how the student's ideas connected to course themes and by framing the thesis as 'specific and takes a real position' (1.3). The dialogue progressed without repetition or drift (2.1, 2.2). Responses were concise and interactive throughout (3.1), and tone was warm, encouraging, and non-evaluative (3.2).
